meta.title: Psychosocial Risk Assessment — ISO 45003 / HSE Management Standards
meta.subtitle: Night-shift contact-centre advisers, Site B
meta.author: HSE Operations
meta.classification: Confidential — special-category data — competent-person review required
meta.deid_notice: Respondents are referred to by role/group label only. Survey, focus-group and sickness-absence data is special-category health data; identifiers were pseudonymised before drafting, any breakdown of fewer than 5 respondents was suppressed with secondary suppression, and the re-identification key is held separately and is not part of this document. No individual is named as the risk.

sections.text: This psychosocial risk assessment covers the night-shift contact-centre advisers at Site B (a named work-design population), against ISO 45003:2021 and the UK HSE Management Standards. It assesses the hazard at source — work design, not the individual — following a recent restructure. Domains in scope: demands, control, support.

items: Framework: ISO 45003:2021 + HSE Management Standards (demands · control · support · relationships · role · change).
Data triangulated across three sources (≥2 required): validated HSE Indicator Tool survey, aggregated focus-group themes, and stress-related sickness-absence trend.
Each domain banded against the HSE Management Standards Indicator Tool benchmark (source + year quoted); rated on the org 5×5 matrix via the risk_matrix engine.
Special-category data: all breakdowns of fewer than 5 respondents suppressed with secondary suppression; no individual named.

rows.title: Demands — sustained workload above achievable levels
rows.description: Night-shift call volumes and after-call work exceed achievable workload for the role-group; survey 'demands' scores in the top-20% (urgent) band and focus-group themes corroborate.
rows.evidence: HSE Indicator Tool survey (top-20% band) + focus-group theme (aggregated)

rows.title: Control — low decision latitude over work pace
rows.description: Advisers report little say over scheduling and adherence targets; survey 'control' below-average band.

rows.title: Support — weakened manager support after restructure
rows.description: Reduced overnight supervisory cover since the restructure; sickness-absence trend rising for the role-group (aggregated, ≥5).

rows: Top-20% urgent — HSE MS Indicator Tool, 2026
Below average — HSE MS Indicator Tool, 2026
Below average — HSE MS Indicator Tool, 2026
sections.caption: Each domain rated on the org 5×5 via risk_matrix; residual re-scored with work-design controls applied.

rows.control: Re-balance night-shift call volumes / after-call work to an achievable workload (staffing + queue routing)
Give advisers decision latitude over breaks and pacing within service levels
Restore overnight supervisory cover and a manager-support route
Manager training on supportive overnight supervision (organisational, not individual resilience)

sections.text: Individual resilience / EAP referral is offered as a SUPPORT measure of last resort — never the primary control. The primary treatment is the work-design controls above (achievable workloads, decision latitude, manager support).

rows.action: Re-balance night-shift workload and confirm achievable staffing
Pilot adviser-controlled break/pacing within service levels
Reinstate overnight supervisory cover + manager-support route

sections.text: This assessment relies on special-category psychosocial data (survey, focus-group, sickness-absence). All respondents are reported as role/group labels; no breakdown of fewer than 5 respondents is published; secondary suppression is applied; the re-identification key is held separately. No individual is named as the risk. Do not widen distribution beyond the named recipients without a further de-identification review.

This is decision-support only and must be reviewed and signed off by a competent person (an occupational-health / psychosocial specialist). Regulatory basis: ISO 45003:2021; UK HSE Management Standards; ISO 45001 §6.1.2 (hazard identification); the jurisdiction's general risk-assessment duty (UK MHSWR 1999 reg. 3). Next review: on change (post-restructure re-assessment) or annual.

items: UK HSE Management Standards — six domains
